source: CIS GOOGLE CLOUD PLATFORM FOUNDATION BENCHMARK V4.0.0
title: Ensure That RDP Access Is Restricted From the Internet

input_row.source: CIS GOOGLE CLOUD PLATFORM FOUNDATION BENCHMARK V4.0.0
input_row.title: Ensure That RDP Access Is Restricted From the Internet
input_row.description: GCP Firewall Rules are specific to a VPC Network. Each rule either allows or denies traffic when its conditions are met. Its conditions allow users to specify the type of traffic, such as ports and protocols, and the source or destination of the traffic, including IP addresses, subnets, and instances. Firewall rules are defined at the VPC network level and are specific to the network in which they are defined. The rules themselves cannot be shared among networks. Firewall rules only support IPv4 traffic. When specifying a source for an ingress rule or a destination for an egress rule by address, an IPv4 address or IPv4 block in CIDR notation can be used. Generic (0.0.0.0/0) incoming traffic from the Internet to a VPC or VM instance using RDP on Port 3389 can be avoided.
input_row.rationale: GCP Firewall Rules within a VPC Network. These rules apply to outgoing (egress) traffic from instances and incoming (ingress) traffic to instances in the network. Egress and ingress traffic flows are controlled even if the traffic stays within the network (for example, instance-to-instance communication). For an instance to have outgoing Internet access, the network must have a valid Internet gateway route or custom route whose destination IP is specified. This route simply defines the path to the Internet, to avoid the most general (0.0.0.0/0) destination IP Range specified from the Internet through RDP with the default Port 3389. Generic access from the Internet to a specific IP Range should be restricted. Impact: All Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP) connections from outside of the network to the concerned VPC(s) will be blocked. There could be a business need where secure shell access is required from outside of the network to access resources associated with the VPC. In that case, specific source IP(s) should be mentioned in firewall rules to white- list access to RDP port for the concerned VPC(s).
input_row.audit: From Google Cloud Console 1. Go to VPC network. 2. Go to the Firewall Rules. 3. Ensure Port is not equal to 3389 and Action is not Allow. 4. Ensure IP Ranges is not equal to 0.0.0.0/0 under Source filters. From Google Cloud CLI gcloud compute firewall-rules list -- format=table'(name,direction,sourceRanges,allowed)' Ensure that there is no rule matching the below criteria: • SOURCE_RANGES is 0.0.0.0/0 • AND DIRECTION is INGRESS • AND IPProtocol is TCP or ALL • AND PORTS is set to 3389 or range containing 3389 or Null (not set) Note: • When ALL TCP ports are allowed in a rule, PORT does not have any value set (NULL) • When ALL Protocols are allowed in a rule, PORT does not have any value set (NULL)
input_row.remediation: From Google Cloud Console 1. Go to VPC Network. 2. Go to the Firewall Rules. 3. Click the Firewall Rule to be modified. 4. Click Edit. 5. Modify Source IP ranges to specific IP. 6. Click Save. From Google Cloud CLI 1.Update RDP Firewall rule with new SOURCE_RANGE from the below command: gcloud compute firewall-rules update FirewallName --allow=[PROTOCOL[:PORT[- PORT]],...] --source-ranges=[CIDR_RANGE,...] References: 1. https://cloud.google.com/vpc/docs/firewalls#blockedtraffic 2. https://cloud.google.com/blog/products/identity-security/cloud-iap-enables- context-aware-access-to-vms-via-ssh-and-rdp-without-bastion-hosts Additional Information: Currently, GCP VPC only supports IPV4; however, Google is already working on adding IPV6 support for VPC. In that case along with source IP range 0.0.0.0, the rule should be checked for IPv6 equivalent ::/0 as well.
input_row.impact: All Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP) connections from outside of the network to the concerned VPC(s) will be blocked. There could be a business need where secure shell access is required from outside of the network to access resources associated with the VPC. In that case, specific source IP(s) should be mentioned in firewall rules to white- list access to RDP port for the concerned VPC(s).
input_row.references: 1. https://cloud.google.com/vpc/docs/firewalls#blockedtraffic 2. https://cloud.google.com/blog/products/identity-security/cloud-iap-enables- context-aware-access-to-vms-via-ssh-and-rdp-without-bastion-hosts Additional Information: Currently, GCP VPC only supports IPV4; however, Google is already working on adding IPV6 support for VPC. In that case along with source IP range 0.0.0.0, the rule should be checked for IPv6 equivalent ::/0 as well.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**
- This compliance check pertains to GCP VPC firewall rule settings, specifically concerning the restriction of RDP traffic (port 3389) from the internet (IP range 0.0.0.0/0).
- The audit instructions specify using the Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) CLI, which indicates the availability of API calls to verify this setting.
- GCP APIs, including Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) commands for managing and inspecting firewall rules, allow automated checks for specific configurations such as ingress rules permitting traffic on RDP port from public IPs.
- The requirement does not need human judgment or instance access, fitting well within the scope of automation provided by Google Cloud SDK (gcloud).

**PROGRAM NAME:** gcp_vpc_firewall_rdp_access_restriction

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- Use the Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) command: `gcloud compute firewall-rules list -- format=table'(name,direction,sourceRanges,allowed)'`.
- Check each firewall rule for the following conditions:
  - `DIRECTION` is `INGRESS`
  - `SOURCE_RANGES` includes `0.0.0.0/0` 
  - `IPProtocol` is `TCP` or `ALL`
  - `PORTS` include `3389` or the ports setting is `NULL` (indicating all ports are allowed)
- Specific resources to enumerate: All firewall rules in the project.
- Pass/Fail Criteria:
  - **Pass:** No firewall rule allows ingress traffic on TCP port 3389 from `0.0.0.0/0`.
  - **Fail:** Any firewall rule permitting ingress traffic on TCP port 3389 from a source range of `0.0.0.0/0`.
- The check ensures no generic RDP access from the internet, fulfilling the control's compliance requirement. 

**SOURCE:** CIS GOOGLE CLOUD PLATFORM FOUNDATION BENCHMARK V4.0.0
step2_review:
---
### 1. APPROACH VALIDATION: **AGREE**

- **Manual vs Automated Classification:** Correct. This control can and should be checked via automation.
- **GCP API/SDK/CLI Capability:** GCP APIs and the Google Cloud SDK (`gcloud`) fully support listing and inspecting firewall rules, including all required fields (direction, source ranges, allowed protocols/ports).
- **Other Automation Options:** In addition to `gcloud`, this check could also be implemented using the REST API (`compute.firewalls.list`), Terraform state inspection, or third-party tools leveraging GCP APIs. GCP Config (now called "Security Command Center" for some orgs) can also automate this check.
- **CloudFormation:** Not applicable to GCP (AWS-specific).

**Conclusion:** The assessment correctly classifies this as an automated control, and the use of GCP CLI/API is appropriate.

---

### 2. TECHNICAL ACCURACY: **Score: 8/10**

**Strengths:**
- The use of `gcloud compute firewall-rules list` is valid and appropriate.
- The logic for checking `INGRESS`, `0.0.0.0/0`, protocol/port is sound.
- The pass/fail criteria are clear and correct.

**Weaknesses:**
- The provided `gcloud` command is missing some critical details:
  - The `--format` flag is miswritten: `-- format=table'(name,direction,sourceRanges,allowed)'` should be `--format="table(name,direction,sourceRanges,allowed)"` (no space after `--` and correct quoting).
  - The `allowed` field is a list of protocol:ports (e.g., `tcp:3389`), so parsing must handle multiple entries and wildcards (e.g., `tcp:all`, `all:all`).
  - The check should also consider rules where `allowed` is `all` or `tcp` with no ports specified (meaning all TCP ports).
  - The check should explicitly ignore rules with more restrictive source ranges (e.g., internal-only) and only flag `0.0.0.0/0`.
- **Program Name:** `gcp_vpc_firewall_rdp_access_restriction` is descriptive and follows the convention, but could be more explicit (see below).

---

### 3. IMPROVEMENTS

**a. Command Syntax:**
- Correct the `gcloud` command syntax:
  ```sh
  gcloud compute firewall-rules list --format="table(name,direction,sourceRanges,allowed)"
  ```
- For automation, consider using `--format=json` for easier parsing.

**b. Logic Enhancements:**
- Ensure the script checks:
  - `allowed` entries for `tcp:3389`, `tcp:all`, `all:all`, or `tcp` with no ports.
  - Both explicit and implicit "all ports" cases.
- Ensure rules with `disabled: true` are ignored (as they are not active).
- Consider rules with multiple `sourceRanges` (e.g., `0.0.0.0/0,10.0.0.0/8`)—must flag if any include `0.0.0.0/0`.

**c. Alternative API Methods:**
- The REST API (`compute.firewalls.list`) can be used for more scalable or integrated automation.
- Security Command Center and Forseti Security can also perform this check at scale.
- For IaC, Terraform state or GCP Config can be used to enforce and audit this control.

**d. Program Name Suggestion:**
- For clarity and specificity, consider:  
  `gcp_vpc_firewall_ingress_rdp_internet_restriction`
- This makes it clear the check is for ingress, RDP, and internet exposure.

---

### 4. CONFIDENCE: **HIGH**

- The assessment is fundamentally correct, with only minor technical improvements needed for full automation robustness.

---

### 5. FINAL RECOMMENDATION: **MODIFY**

- **Keep the automated classification.**
- **Modify the automation details** as per above:
  - Correct `gcloud` command syntax.
  - Clarify logic for parsing `allowed` and `sourceRanges`.
  - Recommend using JSON output for automation.
  - Suggest more explicit program name.
- **Document edge cases** (e.g., disabled rules, multiple source ranges, all-port wildcards).

---
